draft.introduction: 2 Clement (c. 140 CE) is the earliest surviving Christian sermon, traditionally attributed to Clement of Rome though likely from an unknown author. It urges repentance and good works, with a remarkably high Christology for its date.

translation.text: Brothers, we ought to think of Jesus Christ as we think of God, as the judge of the living and the dead. And we ought not to think little of our salvation. For when we think little of him, we also hope to receive little. And those who listen as though these are small matters do wrong, and we do wrong if we do not know from where and by whom and to what place we were called, and how much suffering Jesus Christ endured for our sake.
translation.original_text: ἀδελφοί, οὕτως δεῖ ἡμᾶς φρονεῖν περὶ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ ὡς περὶ θεοῦ

translator_notes.note: The opening command to think of Christ “as we think of God” (ὡς περὶ θεοῦ) is a direct assertion of Christ’s divinity. Coming from the mid-second century, this shows that high Christology was not a late development of Nicene theology but was present in popular Christian preaching from very early on.
